pages.raw_text:
Copyright, 1904, by the Associated


Farm Press.)


The science of operating the dairy
adjunct to a farm at profit is we'll
illustrated by H. p. Purviance, a pros
porous farmer Of Logan county, III.
He has about 100 head of cows and
calves, and the dairy interest is an
important one in the management of
the farm. The cows are mostly of the
Jersey breed, although there are few
Shorthorns, and some of mixed breed.


The dairy produces on an average
about 200 pounds of butter weekly, in
addition to that consumed on the table
of the family of u. The income is
$2l3 per annum, or $7S per COW of 40
in the herd. Mr. and Mrs. Purviance
argue that the skim milk and butter
milk, some of which is sold and the
rest fed to pigs and calves, cover the
item of labor in connection with the
dairy.


Careful estimates show that the cost
of cows feed each year is about SIS,
leaving a balance of $50 as net profit.
In addition they figure that the calves
produced pay good interest on the in
yestment in the herd and equipment
of the dairy. On the credit side of
the ledger must also be considered the
value to the land of the manure.


All the clover hay is fed during the
winter-as much as the cows will eat
in addition to from a gallon to a gal
Ion and half Of corn bran, the latter
being mixed with wheat. Less bran
is fed when the clover is extra good.
More bran is used if timothy hay is
fed. Less ground feed is used when
on stalk feed.


At the age of three days the calves
are taken from the cows and fed whole
milk from its own mother for three
weeks, then skim milk mixed with
whole milk, the amount being eradu
ally increased until at the age four
or five weeks. skim milk only = Fed.
The calf will then commence to nibbie
hay or grass. Ninety per cent Of the
cows raise a calf annually. The steer
calves are sold as feeders when they
reach the age Of two to three years old.
A little grain is fed during the first
winter, and during the second winter
they 60 through on roughage.


The butter produced at the Purvi-
ance dairy is sold and delivered to pri-
vate families in Lincoln, and even
when occasional lots are sold TO stores
it brings the uaifnrs. nrice Of 80 cents
per pound.
